name: Azazel
description: Azazel occupies a complex and contested position within Abrahamic traditions, primarily emerging in early Jewish literature. His identity ranges from a demonic figure to a scapegoat recipient, reflecting evolving theological perspectives. In Leviticus 16, Azazel is associated with the scapegoat ritual on Yom Kippur, where one goat is sacrificed to Yahweh and another is sent into the wilderness 'for Azazel,' bearing the sins of the Israelites. This ritual interpretation has fueled debates about Azazel's nature. Later apocalyptic and pseudepigraphic texts, such as the Book of Enoch, portray Azazel as a fallen angel who taught humanity forbidden knowledge, including weaponry and cosmetics, thereby corrupting them and leading to divine punishment. This narrative casts him as a rebellious figure responsible for introducing evil into the world, a stark contrast to the scapegoat interpretation. Consequently, Azazel remains a multifaceted and enigmatic figure, embodying both the expiation of sin and the instigation of corruption within the Abrahamic religious landscape.
subtitle: Scapegoat or Fallen Angel

keyMyths.title: The Scapegoat Ritual
keyMyths.description: On Yom Kippur, two goats were selected. One was sacrificed to Yahweh, while the other was designated 'for Azazel.' This second goat was driven into the wilderness, symbolically carrying the sins of the people away. This ritual aimed to purify the community and atone for transgressions. The destination and recipient of the goat, Azazel, remains a topic of scholarly debate, with interpretations ranging from a demonic entity to a remote location.

keyMyths.title: Azazel's Fall in Enoch
keyMyths.description: In the Book of Enoch, Azazel is depicted as one of the fallen angels who descended to Earth and taught humanity forbidden arts. He revealed the secrets of weaponry, metalworking, and cosmetics, leading to increased violence, corruption, and moral decay. His actions angered God and contributed to the divine decision to send the Great Flood to cleanse the Earth of wickedness. This narrative establishes Azazel as a significant source of evil.

keyMyths.title: Imprisonment and Punishment
keyMyths.description: Following his transgression, Azazel and the other fallen angels were punished by God. Azazel was bound hand and foot and cast into a dark abyss, where he would remain until the Day of Judgment. This imprisonment serves as a testament to God's power and justice, emphasizing the consequences of rebellion and the ultimate triumph of good over evil. His eventual release is not explicitly described, leaving his ultimate fate ambiguous.

sources.author: Margaret Barker
sources.work: The Great Angel: A Study of Israel's Second God
sources.description: Explores the concept of a 'second god' in early Jewish thought, offering insights into figures like Azazel.
sources.text: Explores the concept of a 'second god' in early Jewish thought, offering insights into figures like Azazel.

sources.author: Martti Nissinen

sources.description: Examines the book of Enoch, including the sins of the Watchers and Azazel's role in the corruption of humanity.
